questions.question: What is the main concept described by John Nash in the context of game theory?
options: A) Pareto Optimality
B) Nash Equilibrium
C) Mixed Strategies
D) Game Trees
questions.explanation: A state in which no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy, assuming all other players keep their strategies unchanged.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios represents a Nash Equilibrium?
options: A) Both players choose option A
B) Both players choose option B
C) Player 1 chooses option A and Player 2 chooses option B
D) Both players choose option C
questions.explanation: When the best response of one player is to choose a particular action, and the best response of the other player is to choose the same action.

questions.question: What is the key assumption required to apply the Nash Equilibrium concept?
options: A) All players have complete information
B) All players have perfect information
C) All players have rational behavior
D) All players have perfect rational behavior
questions.explanation: Rational behavior, which means that players make decisions based on their own self-interest and try to maximize their payoffs.

questions.question: Can a Nash Equilibrium occur in a game with multiple Nash Equilibria?
options: A) No
B) Yes
C) Only if the game has multiple players
D) Only if the game has multiple strategies
questions.explanation: Yes, a game can have multiple Nash Equilibria, but the concept of a Nash Equilibrium still holds true.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a non-Nash Equilibrium?
options: A) Both players choose the same option
B) Both players choose different options
C) Player 1 chooses option A and Player 2 chooses option B
D) Player 1 chooses option B and Player 2 chooses option A
questions.explanation: When both players have a way to improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy, assuming all other players keep their strategies unchanged.
